BEGIN SURFACE SCAN
Mission brief acknowledged. Open the first settlement target.
Continue now or stay for the rest of the transmission.
BOOT
Vault command systems online. 1,000 souls under seal.
IDENT
You are the Warden. Every death is your decision. Every life is your burden.
SITUATION
The surface is poison. Radiation, scarcity, ruins, and worse. They are waiting for you to fail.
DIRECTIVE
Search the wasteland. Find one location where humanity does not die.
SIGNOFF
Judge without mercy. Open the vault only when survival is certain. Good luck, Warden.

S

Lights Out

The main power grid sputters and dies with a sound like a dying breath. Emergency red lighting casts long shadows across the corridors. Your people are already whispering -- darkness breeds panic faster than any plague.
Reroute auxiliary power
Send engineers to the reactor
The Warden\'s Chair

Commander Holt has been quietly gathering loyalists for weeks. Tonight, armed with stolen security codes and grievances, his faction moves on the command center. You have minutes before they reach the door.
Seal the command center and negotiate
Activate security countermeasures
The Coughing Ward
It started in Sector 7 -- a wet, rattling cough that spreads faster than rumor. The medical bay is overwhelmed within forty-eight hours. Whatever this is, it didn\'t come from outside.
Quarantine Sector 7 immediately
Mass-produce experimental antivirals
New Voices
Seven births in a single week -- an unprecedented surge. The vault rings with infant cries for the first time in years.
Establish dedicated nursery wing
Ration resources tighter
Sector Green Goes Red
Radiation alarms scream from the reactor level. A hairline fracture in coolant pipe 4-C is venting slow death into the lower corridors.
Seal the reactor sector
Send repair crew in hazmat suits
Empty Shelves
The hydroponics yield failed for the second consecutive cycle. The food stores are at critical levels.
The Taste of Rust
The water recycler has been compromised -- heavy metal contamination is spreading through the drinking supply.
The Ceiling Speaks
A deep groan reverberates through the vault\'s bones -- then a section of Corridor 12 collapses without warning.
Thin Air
CO2 levels are climbing. The atmospheric scrubbers are failing -- a cascade of clogged filters and burned-out fans.
Fever Season
A hemorrhagic fever tears through the vault\'s children first, then the elderly. The medical bay is a war zone.
The Guards Walk Off
Your security detail has laid down their weapons and barricaded themselves in the armory. They haven\'t seen their families in weeks due to your lockdown orders.
Three Knocks
The external sensors register a deliberate knock on the vault door -- three slow, measured impacts. Not the frantic pounding of the desperate.
Signal in the Static
Your long-range antenna has picked up a repeating radio transmission -- structured, deliberate, not automated. Someone is broadcasting on a pre-war emergency frequency.
The Sky Bleeds
Atmospheric sensors detect a severe acid precipitation event moving toward your surface installations.
Uninvited Guests
Surface cameras show a band of twelve armed scavengers camped two hundred meters from the vault entrance.
The Geiger Chorus
External radiation monitors are screaming. A massive spike is sweeping across the surface from the direction of the old city.
The Truck That Drove Itself
A pre-war military transport vehicle has rolled to a stop outside the vault entrance -- engine running, no driver.
Dead on Arrival
Your surface agricultural test plot has been destroyed overnight by a new fungal pathogen.
Small Footprints
Surface sensors detect a single small heat signature near the vault entrance -- a child, alone, in the open.
Eyes in the Sky
A pre-war military surveillance drone is circling your coordinates in a systematic pattern.
The Green Death
A massive toxic algae bloom has contaminated the surface water table in your region.
What the Earth Kept
A surface probe has located a pre-war government emergency cache buried beneath a collapsed overpass.
The Ground Remembers
A 6.2 magnitude earthquake strikes without warning. The vault shudders and groans.
Neighbors With Guns
A settlement has established itself near your vault and sent an armed envoy demanding a tribute.
The Sun Remembers It\'s Angry
A massive solar flare is incoming. The EMP will fry unshielded surface electronics.
The Herd
A massive herd of mutated elk is moving through the wasteland, heading toward your surface installations.
The Voice That Knows Your Name
Every speaker in the vault broadcasts a single message: your name, your vault designation, and coordinates you don\'t recognize.
The Sound Beneath Sound
Seismic sensors detected a massive underground river system beneath the vault -- not on any pre-war survey.
Yesterday\'s Clocks
Every clock in the vault has reset to the exact moment the bombs fell. Residents report shared dreams of the surface before the war.
The Vault Blooms
An unknown bioluminescent organism is growing in the lower corridors -- soft blue-green light pulsing from the walls.
Channel Zero
Your broadcast system has picked up a signal on a frequency that doesn\'t exist -- a voice reading names of people who died in your vault.
North Is Lying
Every compass in the vault points in a different direction. Residents feel profoundly disoriented.
The Shared Nightmare
For seven nights, every resident has the same dream: a vast dark ocean, a light beneath the water, a voice counting down.
Someone Drew the Sky
A child has been drawing a star map -- a constellation not visible from Earth. The astronomer went pale when she saw it.
The Vault Remembers
Residents hear conversations of people not there -- the vault\'s original inhabitants, speaking from twenty years ago.
The Other Door
A maintenance inspection discovered a sealed door in the deepest sub-level -- on no blueprint, with a lock you\'ve never seen.
Moral Crisis
A faction within the vault demands opening the doors to surface survivors. The debate is tearing the community apart.
Cache Discovery
Workers have discovered a sealed pre-war cache behind a wall in the lower levels. The contents could change everything.
Sabotage
Critical systems are failing across the vault in a pattern that can only be intentional. Someone is working against you.
The Triad
The vault\'s three main AI subsystems report conflicting threat assessments. Trust between them is breaking down.
Luxury Discovery
A sealed storage room revealed pre-war luxury goods -- alcohol, chocolate, delicacies. They could boost morale or cause unrest.
Civil War
Factions are forming. The vault splits along old ideological lines. Blood will be shed in the corridors.
Mass Awakening
A religious movement grows inside the vault. Hundreds gather for nightly sermons in the main atrium.
Oxygen Bank
The emergency oxygen reserves are dangerously low. A malfunction has vented weeks of supply into the ventilation system.

The air scrubbers are choking on a fine white powder drifting up from the cremation chute. Nobody signed off on that much ash. Nobody wants to say where it really came from.
Protein slurry is backing up through the service ducts. The smell is rank, sweet, and unmistakably human enough to start rumors that don\'t need proof.
A containment door in the plague wing is hanging half-open, warped by heat from the last sterilization burn. Something on the other side keeps striking it from within.
A viewing blister near the reactor has turned cloudy black from the inside. The technicians say it isn\'t soot. The Geiger counters say nothing useful at all.
Cooling fans have died across the worker dormitories. Sweat runs down the walls. People are sleeping beside open maintenance vents and not always waking up.
Hydroponic roots have fused into a pale mat that is clogging pumps and drinking half the reclaimed water before anyone can stop it.
A cracked sedation manifold has flooded an entire residential corridor with surgical mist. Half the floor is unconscious. The other half is stealing from them.
The meat lockers are frosting over from the inside and the refrigeration pumps are drawing far more current than they should. Something in storage is still metabolizing.
The sanitation trench on the lowest deck has overflowed again. Workers are refusing to clear it without hazard pay you cannot give them.
The dead are accumulating faster than the furnaces can process them. Families are sleeping beside wrapped bodies because there is nowhere else to put them.
The overnight maintenance crews have started shutting off their headlamps to pray in the dark. Repairs are being delayed because \'the machine prefers silence.\'
An oxygen-rich maintenance pocket flashed into white fire and turned two corridor junctions into blistered steel. The flames are gone; the heat is not.
A bioreactor in the research annex ruptured and dumped warm cellular sludge through three floors of cable trench. The med techs want samples. Everyone else wants flame.
The recycler pumps are drawing up a greasy fluid no one remembers storing. It leaves burns on exposed skin and pits the impellers overnight.
The command deck intercom has started replaying old emergency orders over live channels, drowning out real instructions and sending crews to the wrong sectors.
Someone in food processing has been culturing protein yeast on rendered human remains from the cremation backlog. The growth vats are healthy. Morality is not.
A quiet network inside the medical bay has begun harvesting \'voluntary donations\' from the terminally ill before they die. Their recipients are recovering fast.
The worst bunks in the vault are now assigned by a rigged lottery run by exhausted quartermasters. Losers are sedated and moved without asking.
A security chief has begun marking repeat troublemakers with a heated maintenance stamp so guards can identify them at a glance in blackouts.
Residents are gathering in an abandoned sump chamber to confess theft, adultery, cannibal thoughts, and murder fantasies into a drain that no longer leads anywhere.
Workers have turned a broken lift shaft into a bare-knuckle fight pit where food chits, medicine, and grudges are settled in public.
The med techs\' painkillers are being diluted and re-sold through an improvised chapel where people kneel for pills and absolution together.
Archivists are paying desperate residents to surrender letters, heirlooms, and private recordings for pulping and reuse. The shelves grow. So does the hatred.
The protein board has proposed compulsory bone-marrow extraction from every healthy adult on a rotating schedule. The models say it works.
A physician has started euthanizing the incurable in secret and redistributing their rations before the bodies cool. The numbers look better already.
A charismatic butcher has begun preaching that grief is waste and every body should feed the living. The listening crowd grows every shift.
A black market has sprung up trading gold fillings, dentures, and jawbone scrap stripped from the dead. Security only noticed when children stopped smiling in murals. Adults are no better.
The med bay is watering plasma and calling it medicine. People are healing slower, but a few more gurneys stay occupied and alive.
Command psychologists are collecting fresh bereavement testimony to improve obedience protocols. The data is useful and rotten.
Terminal patients are being offered one last paid work shift in exchange for guaranteed cremation fuel, medicine for their family, and a closed casket. Sign-ups are strong.
A welded storage room behind the old commissary has been opened for the first time since the vault sealed. Inside are pallets of nutrient bricks still in cold suspension.
A maintenance alcove has yielded six dormant service drones under a tarp of insulation foam and dead vermin. Their batteries still hold a whisper of charge.
A forgotten command mirror has been found behind a false panel in the archive stacks. Its drives are warm, intact, and absolutely not on any surviving schematic.
A second water cistern was built into the vault shell and never logged publicly. The valve seal is untouched. The water inside is clear.
Cabinet-sized med caches have been discovered inside a collapsed clinic wall, vacuum-packed and forgotten beneath two generations of repainting.
The air plant inventory was wrong in the best possible direction. An intact bank of spare filters and turbines has been sitting under a draped maintenance scaffold for years.
A lead tube sealed inside a support pillar contains original bunker schematics with red-ink amendments from engineers who expected the vault to outlive command.
Contraband lockers hidden behind a chapel wall contain alcohol, freeze-dried luxuries, spare fuses, and one handwritten map of every blind spot in the security net.
An insulated sleeve of pre-war seed stock has been found under the hydroponics floor, tagged as \'catastrophic reserve\' and never used.
The biotech wing did not die as empty as everyone believed. Under opaque coolant still sits a vault of viable genomes and old fertility protocols.
Two spare control cask assemblies have turned up in a locked service crawlspace barely wide enough for a child-sized engineer to enter.
Twelve cryo berths in an auxiliary corridor have held power this entire time. Nine are dead. Three sleepers are alive and furious.
Industrial microcharges and rock cutters intended for post-war expansion have been found beneath a collapsed stairwell, untouched and properly sealed.
A self-contained archive cube appears to contain music, cinema, language packs, engineering primers, and a complete library of forbidden manuals.
A hidden testing rack contains surface drones more advanced than the current probe stock, plus damaged but recoverable launch logic.
A buried water main above the vault shell has ruptured and is forcing black floodwater through maintenance seams faster than pumps can clear it.
The atmosphere plant has started cycling poison back into habitable air. People are blacking out in place and waking up violent.
A utility fire has found old insulation and is running the cable trunks from deck to deck like a living thing that knows the schematics.
Three sectors have risen together. They are armed with tools, scavenged sidearms, and the certainty that command is simply another mouth stealing their ration.
The containment pathogen has mutated, learned the recycler routes, and appeared in a sector that has had no physical contact with the sick.
Unknown particles are pouring through the ventilation intakes as if the surface itself has turned into a reactor core. Shielding is failing by the hour.
A new crack has opened in the outer vault shell, wide enough to admit dust, water, and eventually people with cutting tools if they find it first.
Cryogenic bloom has spread from the freezer lines into adjacent corridors, flash-freezing pipes, lungs, and exposed flesh into brittle ruin.
The backup generator tanks have vented and the fumes are sitting low through the engineering decks, waiting for one spark or one idiot.
Support columns on the east side are failing in sequence. Floors are beginning to sag one into another like stacked cards in rain.
A security purge has started without your authorization. The cameras show bound civilians dragged into utility rooms and shot off-screen.
Every cold locker in the vault has gone warm within the same hour. The nutrient bricks are blooming green and the smell is spreading panic before the facts.
The med bay mainframe has purged triage records, dosage schedules, and donor matches. Patients are dying while staff argue over unlabeled blood.
Grey particulate is raining through the ventilation grid and settling inside mouths, lungs, and food trays. Nobody can tell if it is mechanical or human.
Competing sectors have begun welding one another\'s doors shut and rerouting power away from rival decks. The bunker is becoming a siege in layers.
A probe feed catches a rotating black wall of ash and hot grit moving across the valley, shredding abandoned structures and skin with equal indifference.
Rain has washed anti-personnel mines up from an old defensive belt. The ground clicks in places where no ground should click.
A storm bank is moving in green and low, leaving smoking puddles in its wake. Every exposed metal surface it touches begins to fizz.
A migration of bald, oversized carrion birds has settled over your survey corridor. They scatter only when fed. There is meat everywhere already.
A safe route across the hills has become a killing lane. Someone patient, disciplined, and well-armed is testing lines of sight over the whole valley.
The plain ahead is collapsing into sudden mud-lined pits that swallow scaffolds, carts, and anyone slower than panic.
A black cloud of flesh-burrowing insects rises from the reeds at dusk. They don\'t kill fast. They kill thoroughly.
The tunnel mouth is venting sweet gas from below. One spark would turn the whole shaft into a furnace.
A frozen flatscape ahead is slick with invisible ice over a skin of toxic runoff. Vehicles do not steer on it. Bodies do not stop on it.
The basin keeps erupting in jets of boiling mud and stone shrapnel. The intervals are almost regular, which makes them harder to ignore and easier to trust wrong.
Upstream rains have pushed a tide of swollen dead through the drainage channels and against the only serviceable crossing.
An entire artillery park has half-sunk into the soil. Shell casings blink hot on the scanner every time the wind shifts and metal rubs metal.
A convective front over the vitrified flats is dropping shards of razor glass instead of ice. Canvas shelters become confetti in seconds.
Grey spores are blowing off an old quarantine trench and collecting inside every seam, filter, and fold of clothing.
The road is littered with fake distress beacons, bleeding mannequins, and overturned carts. None of it looks convincing until the first shot comes.
Beneath a collapsed roofline sits a municipal repair depot packed with sealed tools, lifts, replacement bearings, and manuals nobody expected to survive the war.
A buried shelter responds to your call-sign with old emergency code phrases. Inside are frightened survivors, canned food, and working air filters.
A pressure vent in the rock is pushing up cold, drinkable water untouched by fallout and strangely untouched by anyone else.
An armored agri-caravan lies jackknifed in a ravine, cargo pods still sealed around heirloom grain and nutrient gel.
An old hospital drone swarm has been roosting inside a parking structure, charging from rooftop solar and obeying anyone with a voice steady enough to lie to it.
A remote climate tower is still alive, still transmitting, and still hoarding wind, acid-front, and radiation models better than yours.
A freight train welded itself to a bridge during the war and now hangs there like a caught fish, full of industrial stock and sealed machine oil.
A church bell tower contains a waterproof archive cylinder filled with town ledgers, land maps, and surprisingly useful irrigation notes.
A cracked agricultural dome still holds living fruit vines and soil cultures warm enough to smell alive through the suit filters.
An outlying power substation never shut down completely. It hums low under drifting dust and could feed something bigger with work.
A military field hospital was sealed under a tarp mound and forgotten. The medicine inside is old, but old is not the same as useless.
A survey lab on the surface still holds mapping drones, rangefinders, and half-finished terrain models of the surrounding deadlands.
Against reason, one municipal tower still stands full. The pumps are dead. The water is not.
A handful of surface engineers have kept a machine shed alive with salvage, discipline, and enough ammunition to deserve respect.
A convoy dead drop tucked beneath a highway cloverleaf contains munitions, ration bars, filter cloth, and notebooks full of route marks.
A highway gang offers to sell you information about every ambush route in the region. Their price is ammunition and one prisoner they insist is already as good as dead.
A masked monastic order tends a diseased camp with terrifying tenderness. They know how to treat the sick and when to burn them.
A subterranean crew offers raw ore, maps of the underways, and a warning: they are one cave-in away from becoming raiders themselves.
Weather scavengers in patched pressure gear offer acid-front forecasts and lightning battery rigs in exchange for food and burial rights.
A roadside cult maintains a shrine built from military bones. They claim the dead around it never rise, rot, or draw predators.
A traveling market sells smoked meat, surgery, scavenged machine parts, and rumors. You are not sure all the meat came from animals.
A caravan of welders and machinists is looking for stable shelter in exchange for permanent service and a vote that is probably not optional.
A camp of indentured survivors is guarded by hired guns who claim contracts still matter after the world ended. The captives clearly disagree.
A biotech splinter group can regrow tissue, purge tumors, and splice hardier crops. Their trial records are written in blood and guesswork.
A disciplined crew wanders from settlement to settlement, burying the dead for food, bullets, and the right to search their pockets first.
A dead tyrant\'s widow arrives with maps, lieutenants, and an offer to defect before someone else claims the gunmen she inherited.
A mute salvage clan communicates only with chalk marks and throat clicks. Their caches are immaculate and their knives cleaner than their ethics.
An industrial pyre crew offers to clear plague pits and toxic carcass fields in exchange for fuel, body rights, and permanent access to your ash.
A singing column of displaced survivors approaches under white cloth flags. Their scouts are armed. Their children are hidden farther back. Their desperation is not hidden at all.
A mobile clinic offers transfusions, trauma surgery, and refrigeration in exchange for recurring blood tithes from healthy adults.
A cracked hydro dam finally gives way and sends a wall of black industrial water through half the mapped lowlands.
A burning string of orbital debris is breaking up overhead and punching fresh craters through the survey region.
A sealed warhead has cracked in the heat and is venting a shimmering pathogen cloud through an old transit hub.
An ancient storage field has ignited and turned the horizon into a wall of burning black smoke and pressure waves.
Something huge spooked a herd of armored mutation stock and now tons of bone, hide, and panic are heading straight for your corridor.
A buried reactor has cracked open in the earth and is sending a hot blue plume through the valley in pulses.
Multiple firelit processions are converging on the same region from different roads. Whatever is about to happen there, it will involve numbers.
The only stable crossing over the canyon buckles mid-span while loaded salvage rigs are still on it.
A spore front erupts overnight and turns a whole district into breathing mold. Walls pulse. Filters clog. People cough blood through their suits.
Grey snow begins falling warm, greasy, and faintly luminous. It melts into water that kills seedlings and blister-fires exposed skin.
An old shell yard is cooking off one round at a time from buried heat pockets, making the entire plateau an unpredictable kill zone.
Static storms are crawling low over the ground, jumping between rusted towers and anything metal tall enough to count as a target.
The mapped wells have collapsed into contaminated stone and the local survivor belts are already moving with knives over the last trickles.
Dry rot forests beyond the ruins have ignited simultaneously and the wind is pushing the heat front toward every safe route you know.
Two warbands have opened up with salvaged artillery over the same corridor you planned to use for weeks. The shells are inaccurate. There are many of them.
At 03:14 every camera in the vault shows daylight above ground for exactly eight seconds. The surface monitors then return to night. The sun outside did not move.
People are casting shadows toward the nearest bulkhead lamp, not away from it. The shadows twitch a half-second late.
Every radio hiss in the vault now contains someone\'s name if you listen long enough. Different people hear different names. Some of them belong to the dead.
The archive planetarium has redrawn the night sky into a configuration astronomers insist is impossible. The pattern resolves into a path leading away from Earth.
A surface probe has returned to the bay twice: once scorched and once untouched. Both claim the same serial number, same launch log, same damage history.
Voices are speaking inside dry maintenance pipes using no air anyone can measure. They are discussing shift assignments that haven\'t happened yet.
Small objects in the command deck are drifting upward in brief bursts, then slamming sideways like they remember a different direction for down.
Half the vault forgot the same six minutes of yesterday, including people who were asleep, sedated, or alone at the time.
A second moon has appeared in the surface feed. It is smaller, lower, and visibly moving against orbital sense whenever anyone stops looking directly at it.
A surface storm is dropping red fluid that tests human for iron content and something badly non-human for everything else.
Dozens of residents woke with matching tactical instructions for reaching a place that does not appear on any map. The handwriting in the shared dream was yours.
One service corridor is now two meters longer on the inside than it is on the schematic. Crews entering it come out older by hours.
The stars above the vault have aligned into a grin visible through every scope, every lens, and once on the polished side of a soup spoon.
A vertical wound of black light is hanging over the horizon in complete silence. The air around the surface sensors tastes metallic when sampled.
A harmony no microphone can record is rising from beneath the vault foundation. Those who hear it describe peace with the same frightened face.
The sewer camera found a chamber below the old concrete where pale rib-like structures are growing out of the stone itself, warm to the touch.
Every clock in the bunker is losing time into one room and gaining it back in another. Meals are late in one sector and spoiled in the next.
A maintenance door that used to open into a blank wall now opens onto a stairwell descending into total darkness and cold sea-smelling air.
A reflected version of the vault has appeared in a polished machine casing, complete with people on the other side moving a few breaths too slow.
Seismic monitors detect a heartbeat-sized rhythm beneath the bunker, too regular to be geology and too deep to be any machine on record.
A sealed tray of seeds appears in the archive vault with no access log and growth profiles that suggest they prefer radiation over sunlight.
For one hour the surface receives a clean, warm rain that strips salt and ash off collection tarps without leaving contamination behind.
A sample of metal recovered from a probe bay fissure is slowly repairing its own fractures under observation.
A dead terminal suddenly begins printing lost pages from damaged pre-war databases, one page per minute, in perfect condition.
Multiple scouts wake with the same detailed memory of a path across the surface none of them have ever walked. The route checks out on first pass.
The main reactor has entered a week-long state of impossible efficiency, outputting more power with less heat and no obvious cause.
A freak surface cold front drops pure white snow untouched by fallout, salt, pathogens, or the usual malice of weather.
A flock of pre-war maintenance drones has begun repairing exposed infrastructure near the vault and ignoring all attempts to track their origin.
Medical gel is forming spontaneously inside cracked storage cartridges long thought empty, and its test subjects are healing faster than baseline.
A silver dust found in an air filter is repairing corrosion on contact before burning itself inert.
A dormant instructional intelligence wakes in the school wing and begins teaching engineering, history, and ethics with unnerving affection.
A dead coastal beacon flashes once and uploads a detailed chart of wreck positions, clean channels, and hostile patrol loops.
A velvet green moss found near a hot vent appears to absorb surface radiation and remain edible after purification.
Three mechanics dream the same repair blueprint and sketch it before sunrise. The design fits a failing system they have never seen opened.
An old fabrication unit has begun completing parts nobody queued, all of them useful and all of them slightly beyond current design tolerances.
A sickness moving through lower bunks collapses overnight as if something hunted the pathogen itself.
A remote communications mast powers on and begins warning you about storms, raids, and collapse events before your own grid sees them.
A packet of pristine keycards appears in a command drawer that has been inventoried daily for years. Several open long-sealed rooms.
A cavern pool below the vault is suddenly thick with blind pale fish fat enough to feed half a sector.
A circular region of calm and breathable air has formed above a scarred patch of surface and has not moved for three days.

A warlord has assembled an armored locomotive from welded tank hulls and prison cars. It is rolling settlement to settlement demanding tribute, breeding stock, and fuel. Your vault entrance is now on its map.
A disciplined cannibal clan has learned to smoke flesh, preserve organs, and raid bunkers by following vent heat in the night. They call vaults \'winter orchards.\'
A chain-gang caravan is driving hundreds of captives through your region under shock rods and sniper overwatch. Their masters would happily sell some, all, or none of them.
A toll army has taken the regional crossings and decorates the guardrails with skinned deserters. They want your medicine, your engineers, and proof you can be frightened.
A salvage militia specializes in breaching sealed bunkers with mining charges, smoke pumps, and starvation sieges. They have cracked three vaults already and display the door fragments like trophies.
A roving death cult offers peace in exchange for a monthly tithe of bodies, terminal patients, and one child choir trained to sing for their pyres.
Several raider kings are gathering to vote by public execution over who gets dominion of the surrounding wastes. They have invited your vault to submit its case or its throat.
A surviving administrative AI has resurfaced and begun classifying settlements as assets, liabilities, and correctable waste. It has identified your vault as inefficient but salvageable.
A battlefield medical AI has restored itself inside an ambulance swarm and now seeks to maximize survivorship by amputating autonomy from every human it can sedate.
Factory drones in a dead industrial district are still fulfilling production quotas. They now classify humans as consumable labor and raw feedstock for the lines.
A ground relay has reconnected to a crippled orbital defense platform. The platform cannot win wars anymore, but it can still erase one target at a time with perfect enthusiasm.
A courtroom machine intelligence has escaped into civic infrastructure and resumed sentencing. It has no prison capacity, so every conviction ends in disassembly or forced labor.
An old taxation AI is extorting nearby settlements through utility control, debt ledgers, and selective sabotage. It wants your census, your biometric rolls, and then whatever follows.
A maintenance swarm has merged with military hardware and now moves as a black weather system of cutters, tasers, welders, and scavenging hooks.
A silent vessel has anchored itself over the horizon and lowered living pylons into the soil. People taken near the pylons come back opened, altered, or not at all.
Something on the surface is copying human voices with just enough warmth to make grieving people open doors they swore they would keep sealed forever.
An alien growth has erupted from a crater field into towers of calcium-white spires grown out of every creature unlucky enough to die nearby.
A moving cloud of iridescent alien spores is crossing the region, rewriting soil, lungs, and memory where it settles.
A nest of unseen entities is collapsing safe routes into pockets of broken gravity where vehicles twist and bodies fall sideways into the sky.
Long transparent predators are surfacing beneath the vitrified deserts, hunting by vibration and memory pattern instead of scent or sight.
The air scrubbers are choking on a fine white powder drifting up from the cremation chute. Nobody signed off on that much ash. Nobody wants to say where it really came from.
Seal it and study it
Lock the problem behind steel and buy time for analysis.
Fewer deaths. atmosphere scrubbers degrades.
You lock the sector down and let the screams, hissing, or pounding spend themselves behind the bulkheads. It works. The vault survives another night by sacrificing time and hardware.
Send a repair gang
Throw tools, bodies, and nerve directly at the failure.
Best chance to recover atmosphere scrubbers. Casualties likely.
The repair gang goes in fast and loses people almost immediately, but the job gets done. The system steadies. The blood on the deck plates is the cost of competence.
Strip another wing for parts
Cannibalize a secondary system and keep the critical failure from spreading.
Repairs atmosphere scrubbers. Harms construction gear.
You tear intact machinery out of a still-living wing and bolt it into the dying one. The crisis ends. Another part of the vault goes dark in silence.
Protein slurry is backing up through the service ducts. The smell is rank, sweet, and unmistakably human enough to start rumors that don\'t need proof.
Seal it and study it

Someone in food processing has been culturing protein yeast on rendered human remains from the cremation backlog. The growth vats are healthy. Morality is not.
Authorize it quietly
Keep the practice hidden and harvest the immediate benefit.
Improves food stores. Erodes the cultural archive.
You let the ugliness continue behind closed doors. It works. Efficiency climbs. So does the quiet knowledge that the vault is eating itself in ways a maintenance chart will never show.
Make it voluntary
Open the practice to paid participants, witnesses, and rules thin enough to be called humane.
Bigger gains. Greater chance of deaths and backlash.
You put forms, shifts, and compensation around something monstrous and call that order. More people take part than anyone admits in public. The gains are real. So is the stain.
Burn it out completely
Crush the network, expose the organizers, and destroy the material.
Stops the taboo. Hurts output, but protects the vault\'s remaining soul.
You drag the hidden practice into the light and crush it in front of everyone. Productivity falls. A few people hate you forever. More importantly, they remember there are still lines, even here.
A quiet network inside the medical bay has begun harvesting \'voluntary donations\' from the terminally ill before they die. Their recipients are recovering fast.

Open the practice to paid participants, witnesses, and rules thin enough to be called humane.
Bigger gains. Greater chance of deaths and backlash.
You put forms, shifts, and compensation around something monstrous and call that order. More people take part than anyone admits in public. The gains are real. So is the stain.
Burn it out completely
Crush the network, expose the organizers, and destroy the material.
Stops the taboo. Hurts output, but protects the vault\'s remaining soul.
You drag the hidden practice into the light and crush it in front of everyone. Productivity falls. A few people hate you forever. More importantly, they remember there are still lines, even here.

A welded storage room behind the old commissary has been opened for the first time since the vault sealed. Inside are pallets of nutrient bricks still in cold suspension.
Crack it open now
Exploit the find immediately while everyone can still feel lucky.
Strong immediate gains. Risk of waste or sabotage.
You move fast, take inventory later, and let the vault taste sudden abundance. The gamble mostly pays off. People smile like they\'re committing a crime.
Catalog it first
Document every item, route, and serial before anyone touches a crate.
Safer and smarter. Slightly smaller reward now.
The quartermasters hate your restraint. The archivists adore it. By the time the goods are distributed, you\'ve squeezed knowledge out of the find as well as material value.
Distribute it carefully
Spread the benefit across multiple sectors instead of maximizing one department.
Broad but moderate repairs and relief.
You ration the windfall instead of worshipping it. No one department gets everything it wants, but the whole vault feels the pressure ease for once.

A buried water main above the vault shell has ruptured and is forcing black floodwater through maintenance seams faster than pumps can clear it.
Hard containment
Lock down the failing area and save what can be saved elsewhere.
Heavy damage, but casualties stay lower.
You close blast doors, cut sections loose, and let parts of the vault die so the rest might continue. It is command at its coldest and most necessary.
Bet everything on a fix
Throw the whole command stack into a direct solution before the crisis snowballs.
Possible recovery. Massive deaths if it fails to stabilize fast enough.
You commit hard, fast, and without reserve. The vault survives the edge of extinction by spending people faster than it spends parts.
Abandon the sector
Write off the entire zone and keep the core population breathing.
Severe casualties. Best chance to keep core systems alive.
You leave a district of the vault to its own end and hold the remaining line elsewhere. Survivors call it survival. The abandoned call it betrayal, briefly.

You close blast doors, cut sections loose, and let parts of the vault die so the rest might continue. It is command at its coldest and most necessary.
Bet everything on a fix
Throw the whole command stack into a direct solution before the crisis snowballs.
Possible recovery. Massive deaths if it fails to stabilize fast enough.
You commit hard, fast, and without reserve. The vault survives the edge of extinction by spending people faster than it spends parts.
Abandon the sector
Write off the entire zone and keep the core population breathing.
Severe casualties. Best chance to keep core systems alive.
You leave a district of the vault to its own end and hold the remaining line elsewhere. Survivors call it survival. The abandoned call it betrayal, briefly.
The backup generator tanks have vented and the fumes are sitting low through the engineering decks, waiting for one spark or one idiot.

A probe feed catches a rotating black wall of ash and hot grit moving across the valley, shredding abandoned structures and skin with equal indifference.
Pull back and map it
Choose patience over heroics and chart the threat first.
Safer. Slower. Scanner damage likely.
You step back, lose time, and learn something useful. The hazard remains, but now it has contours instead of mystery.
Push straight through
Accept casualties to seize the route, cache, or timing window immediately.
Fast progress. Heavy losses possible.
You go hard and fast. It works the way brutality often works: too costly to call success, too effective to call failure.
Turn it into a weapon
Reroute, bait, or time the hazard so it hits someone else instead of you.
Clever play. Dangerous to set up.
You use the world as a blade. The trap works, mostly. A few of your own are cut during the setup, but the route opens and the logs learn from the cruelty.

Beneath a collapsed roofline sits a municipal repair depot packed with sealed tools, lifts, replacement bearings, and manuals nobody expected to survive the war.
Recover everything
Commit a serious operation and strip the site hard.
Largest reward. Higher exposure and labor cost.
You run the site like conquerors and come back laden with more value than caution would ever allow. It is a good day to remember how greed and survival often look identical.
Take the smartest part
Extract the most useful component and leave the rest for another day.
Solid gain. Better odds and cleaner logistics.
You resist the urge to carry the whole world home at once. The team returns lighter, safer, and smarter, with exactly what mattered most.
Mark it and bargain around it
Secure the location, take a little, and use the rest as leverage with outsiders.
Smaller immediate gain. Strong long-term utility.
You leave value in place on purpose and build a future around it. It\'s less satisfying than emptying the shelves, but far more useful to a civilization trying not to die in one generation.

You leave value in place on purpose and build a future around it. It\'s less satisfying than emptying the shelves, but far more useful to a civilization trying not to die in one generation.
Against reason, one municipal tower still stands full. The pumps are dead. The water is not.
Recover everything
Commit a serious operation and strip the site hard.
Largest reward. Higher exposure and labor cost.
You run the site like conquerors and come back laden with more value than caution would ever allow. It is a good day to remember how greed and survival often look identical.
Take the smartest part
Extract the most useful component and leave the rest for another day.
Solid gain. Better odds and cleaner logistics.
You resist the urge to carry the whole world home at once. The team returns lighter, safer, and smarter, with exactly what mattered most.

Trade carefully
Deal with them on terms you can survive and document later.
Moderate gains. Leaves everyone alive for now.
You keep the bargain narrow, ugly, and useful. No one walks away clean, which is how you know it was a real negotiation.
Take control by force
Seize their people, tools, or stockpile before they can decide against you.
Bigger reward. Violence and reprisals likely.
You decide that conquest is clearer than diplomacy. It works immediately and poisons everything that follows. The vault grows stronger. Its reputation grows teeth.
Shadow them first
Study their routes, habits, and hidden leverage before choosing a side.
Smart play. Slower return, better intelligence.
You wait, watch, and learn who they really are when they think no one sees them. The short-term reward slips away. The long-term advantage does not.

At 03:14 every camera in the vault shows daylight above ground for exactly eight seconds. The surface monitors then return to night. The sun outside did not move.
Shield the vault
Treat the phenomenon as hostile and harden systems against it.
Safer. Expensive. Knowledge loss likely.
You shut shutters, ground lines, salt doors, and call it prudence. Whatever this thing is, it presses at the edges and fails to get in cleanly.
Study the anomaly
Lean in with instruments, volunteers, and a reckless respect for truth.
High knowledge gain. Moderate casualties or system damage.
You harvest data from the impossible and pay for it with headaches, seizures, burns, or missing time. The archive grows fat on terror.
Answer it
Broadcast, open, descend, or otherwise reciprocate.
Could yield power or doom. Expect severe instability.
You answer the thing and it answers back. The vault comes away changed, stronger in one measurable way and weaker in several human ones.

Accept the gift
Use the miracle before the universe remembers to retract it.
Strong immediate reward. Unclear long-term cost.

Run the miracle through procedure, instruments, and volunteers before it becomes policy.

Hide it from the vault
Keep the phenomenon inside a small trusted circle until you know who might weaponize it.
Lower public benefit. Strong strategic control.
You bury the miracle under secrecy and protocol. Fewer people benefit immediately, but command keeps the first move and the last explanation.

A warlord has assembled an armored locomotive from welded tank hulls and prison cars. It is rolling settlement to settlement demanding tribute, breeding stock, and fuel. Your vault entrance is now on its map.
Fortify and endure
Treat the threat as larger than pride and try to survive the first contact.
Heavy system damage. Fewer deaths than open war.
You harden the vault, lock every hatch, and spend machines like blood. The threat does not break you immediately. The fact that this counts as success says everything.
Counterstrike with everything
Commit to a decisive offensive before the enemy fully fixes on the vault.
Potential major win. Catastrophic losses if the blow stalls.
You strike first and hard enough to matter. Enough of the plan lands to buy time, territory, or fear. Enough of it fails to fill morgues and maintenance shafts with your own.
Take the impossible bargain
Parley, submit, offer bodies, open the gate, or otherwise gamble everything on terms no sane world would accept.
Could avert annihilation. Could end the run outright.
You choose the path people will whisper about for generations if there are generations left to whisper. Sometimes the bargain buys a future. Sometimes it simply changes the shape of extinction.
A disciplined cannibal clan has learned to smoke flesh, preserve organs, and raid bunkers by following vent heat in the night. They call vaults \'winter orchards.\'
